---
title: "Wikidata Ogham Sites — Interactive Map"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## About this notebook

This notebook fetches Ogham-stone records from
[Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org) and displays their find-spots on
two interactive [Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) maps, each with an
OpenStreetMap basemap. The first map shows individual stones as
county-coloured markers with pop-up details; the second shows the
same points as a density heatmap, making regional concentration
visible at a glance.

It is a companion to `wikidata-ogham-sites-live.qmd` (bar-chart
overview of the same dataset) and part of an Open Educational
Resource series on knowledge graphs and linked open data. A
local-Python variant of this notebook is available as
`wikidata-ogham-sites-map.ipynb`.

### What you'll learn

- How to extract **geographic coordinates** from a Wikidata SPARQL
  response. Coordinates in Wikidata are stored as
  [WKT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Well-known_text_representation_of_geometry)
  `Point(lon lat)` literals — a string format that needs a small
  parser before the values can be plotted.
- How to build an **interactive web map** from Python in a
  browser-only environment, by bridging from Python to JavaScript and
  rendering through Leaflet.

### Data-context notes

The query in this notebook only returns records that *have*
coordinates (`?item wdt:P625 ?geo`, not `OPTIONAL`). This is the
right call for a map — records without coordinates cannot be
plotted — but it also filters out a non-trivial share of stones,
depending on how well the data is curated at the time you run it.
The overview notebook makes the opposite choice, making coordinates
optional, to give a complete picture of counts per county. The
difference between those two result sets is itself an instructive
thing to look at.

A second subtlety: Wikidata stores coordinates in WGS 84
(`EPSG:4326`), the same coordinate reference system Leaflet and
OpenStreetMap expect. No reprojection is needed. If you later query
an endpoint that uses a different projection (e.g. some national
cultural-heritage registries), you will need to reproject.

### Tooling notes

Rendering an interactive map in a browser-only notebook is slightly
unusual, because the Python libraries most people know for this
(`folium`, `geopandas`, `contextily`) do not work well in Pyodide:
`folium` expects to write HTML files, `geopandas` has heavy binary
dependencies (GDAL, GEOS, PROJ), and `contextily` needs server-side
HTTP. The local `.ipynb` variant of this notebook happily uses
`folium`; the browser variant instead builds an HTML snippet with
embedded Leaflet JavaScript and returns it as the cell's output.

There is one additional browser-specific constraint worth knowing
about: `quarto-live` runs Python inside a **Web Worker**, which has
*no DOM access*. That means `js.document`, `js.eval()` targeting the
DOM, and any direct manipulation of page elements from Python will
fail with `ReferenceError: document is not defined`. The working
pattern is the one used below: Python *builds* an HTML string
containing the map container and the Leaflet-initialisation script,
then returns it as the cell value. `quarto-live` inserts that HTML
into the page on the main thread, where the browser has full DOM
access and runs the embedded JavaScript normally.

::: {.callout-note}
On first load, your browser downloads the Python runtime (Pyodide, ~10 MB)
and the Leaflet library (~150 KB). Please allow a moment for these
to initialise.
:::

## Step 1 — Defining the SPARQL query

We ask Wikidata for every Ogham stone, its find-spot, its county, and
its coordinate location. The structure is almost identical to the
overview notebook, with the one difference noted above: coordinates
are required.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: setup
import json
import urllib.parse
from pyodide.http import pyfetch
import pandas as pd

SPARQL_ENDPOINT = "https://query.wikidata.org/sparql"

SPARQL_QUERY = """
SELECT ?item ?itemLabel ?geo ?site ?siteLabel ?county ?countyLabel WHERE {
  ?item wdt:P31 wd:Q2016147.
  ?item wdt:P189 ?site.
  ?site wdt:P31 wd:Q72617071.
  ?item wdt:P189 ?county.
  ?county wdt:P31 wd:Q179872.
  ?item wdt:P625 ?geo.
  SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language "en". }
}
"""

async def fetch_wikidata(query, endpoint=SPARQL_ENDPOINT):
    """Retrieve SPARQL results from a Wikibase endpoint (browser-compatible)."""
    full_url = endpoint + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(
        {"query": query, "format": "json"}
    )
    response = await pyfetch(
        full_url,
        headers={
            "Accept": "application/sparql-results+json",
            "User-Agent": "OER-Notebook/1.0",
        },
    )
    data = await response.json()
    return data["results"]["bindings"]

def parse_wkt_point(wkt):
    """Parse a WKT Point literal 'Point(lon lat)' into (lat, lon)."""
    if not wkt:
        return (None, None)
    try:
        coords = wkt.replace("Point(", "").replace(")", "").split()
        lon, lat = float(coords[0]), float(coords[1])
        return (lat, lon)
    except (ValueError, IndexError):
        return (None, None)

print("✓ Helpers defined. Run the next cell to load the data.")
```

## Step 2 — Loading the data

The `parse_wkt_point` helper defined above converts each `Point(lon lat)`
literal into a `(lat, lon)` tuple. Note the ordering: WKT is
`lon lat`, but Leaflet (and most mapping libraries) expect
`lat, lon`. Getting this the wrong way round is one of the most
common mistakes when plotting spatial data — the kind of bug where
everything "works" but all your points end up in the wrong
hemisphere.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: load-data
results = await fetch_wikidata(SPARQL_QUERY)

rows = []
for r in results:
    lat, lon = parse_wkt_point(r.get("geo", {}).get("value"))
    rows.append({
        "item":        r["item"]["value"],
        "itemLabel":   r["itemLabel"]["value"],
        "site":        r["site"]["value"],
        "siteLabel":   r["siteLabel"]["value"],
        "county":      r["county"]["value"],
        "countyLabel": r["countyLabel"]["value"],
        "latitude":    lat,
        "longitude":   lon,
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows).dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"])
print(f"✓ {len(df)} records with coordinates loaded "
      f"({df['itemLabel'].nunique()} unique Ogham stones "
      f"across {df['countyLabel'].nunique()} counties).")
df.head()
```

## Step 3a — Map with county-coloured markers

We build the first map as an HTML string in Python and return it as
the cell's value via a `_repr_html_`-reprable object. `quarto-live`
inserts the HTML on the main thread, where the browser happily runs
the embedded `<script>` with full DOM access.

This first map loads only core Leaflet, plus the
[`Leaflet.fullscreen`](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet.fullscreen)
plugin for the maximise-to-fullscreen button under the zoom controls.

A [layer control](https://leafletjs.com/examples/layers-control/) in
the top-right corner (expand it by hovering or tapping) offers four
base layers — OpenStreetMap as the default, the Humanitarian OSM
style, Esri satellite imagery, and Esri's world terrain base — and
one overlay *per county*, each with its colour swatch in the label.
Unchecking a county hides just its markers, so you can isolate
regions interactively. All provider URLs and attributions are taken
from the
[leaflet-providers registry](https://github.com/leaflet-extras/leaflet-providers).

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: render-markers-map
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

import uuid

# --- 1. Assign a colour to each county (reused across both maps) ---------
palette = [
    "#e6194B", "#3cb44b", "#4363d8", "#f58231", "#911eb4",
    "#42d4f4", "#f032e6", "#469990", "#9A6324", "#800000",
    "#808000", "#000075", "#e6beff", "#fabed4", "#aaffc3",
    "#fffac8", "#dcbeff", "#ffd8b1", "#a9a9a9", "#ffe119",
]
counties = sorted(df["countyLabel"].unique())
county_colors = {c: palette[i % len(palette)] for i, c in enumerate(counties)}

# --- 2. Prepare records as plain JSON-serialisable dicts -----------------
markers = [
    {
        "lat":    float(row["latitude"]),
        "lon":    float(row["longitude"]),
        "name":   row["itemLabel"],
        "site":   row["siteLabel"],
        "county": row["countyLabel"],
        "color":  county_colors[row["countyLabel"]],
        "url":    row["item"],
    }
    for _, row in df.iterrows()
]

# Sorted county names paired with colours — JS will build one layer per county
county_list = [{"county": c, "color": county_colors[c]} for c in counties]
markers_payload = json.dumps({"markers": markers, "counties": county_list})

# --- 3. A unique id so re-runs don't collide with previous renders -------
markers_map_id = f"ogham-markers-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
markers_data_var = f"__oghamMarkersData_{markers_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

# --- 4. Build the HTML + JS string ---------------------------------------
markers_html = f"""
<div id="{markers_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{markers_data_var}"] = {markers_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{markers_map_id}";
    const data = window["{markers_data_var}"];

    // Load Leaflet.fullscreen dynamically AFTER core Leaflet is ready.
    // Same pattern as the heatmap plugin: avoid the race where a parallel
    // <script> tag evaluates before L is defined.
    function loadFullscreenPlugin(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreenPlugin(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    let initAttempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;  // 40 × 50ms = 2 seconds total grace period

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        // Fullscreen plugin is optional; wait briefly, then proceed without it
        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && initAttempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            initAttempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers (one active at a time) ------------------------
        // URLs and attributions taken from the leaflet-providers registry:
        // https://github.com/leaflet-extras/leaflet-providers
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors, '
                + 'Tiles style by <a href="https://www.hotosm.org/" target="_blank">Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team</a> '
                + 'hosted by <a href="https://openstreetmap.fr/" target="_blank">OpenStreetMap France</a>'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: Esri, i-cubed, USDA, USGS, AEX, GeoEye, '
                + 'Getmapping, Aerogrid, IGN, IGP, UPR-EGP, and the GIS User Community'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,  // Esri caps World_Terrain_Base at zoom 13
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // --- Overlays: one layer per county ---------------------------
        // Each county gets its own L.layerGroup so it can be toggled
        // independently. The marker colour comes from the same palette
        // used elsewhere in the notebook.
        const countyLayers = {{}};
        const allBounds = [];
        for (const c of data.counties) {{
            countyLayers[c.county] = L.layerGroup();
        }}
        for (const m of data.markers) {{
            const marker = L.circleMarker([m.lat, m.lon], {{
                radius: 6, color: m.color, weight: 1.5,
                fillColor: m.color, fillOpacity: 0.75
            }}).bindPopup(
                '<b>' + m.name + '</b><br>' +
                'Site: ' + m.site + '<br>' +
                'County: ' + m.county + '<br>' +
                '<a href="' + m.url + '" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Wikidata entry &#8599;</a>'
            );
            marker.addTo(countyLayers[m.county]);
            allBounds.push([m.lat, m.lon]);
        }}

        // --- Create map, show OSM + all county layers by default -------
        // Build an HTML legend swatch inside each overlay label so the
        // layer-control doubles as a colour key.
        const initialLayers = [osm];
        const overlays = {{}};
        for (const c of data.counties) {{
            initialLayers.push(countyLayers[c.county]);
            const swatch = '<span style="display:inline-block;width:10px;height:10px;'
                + 'background:' + c.color + ';border-radius:50%;'
                + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>';
            overlays[swatch + c.county] = countyLayers[c.county];
        }}

        const mapOptions = {{
            center: [53.0, -8.0], zoom: 7,
            layers: initialLayers
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        if (allBounds.length) map.fitBounds(allBounds, {{ padding: [30, 30] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        // collapsed:true → Leaflet default, expands on hover / tap
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreenPlugin(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

class _Html:
    def __init__(self, s): self._s = s
    def _repr_html_(self): return self._s

_Html(markers_html)
```

## Step 3b — Map with density heatmap

The second map shows the same data as a density heatmap, which makes
regional concentration immediately obvious. The heatmap is rendered
by [`leaflet.heat`](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet.heat), and
[`Leaflet.fullscreen`](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet.fullscreen)
adds the maximise-to-fullscreen button. The same four base layers as
in Step 3a are offered; the heatmap is the overlay.

One subtlety worth pointing out: Leaflet plugins must be loaded
*after* core Leaflet, because they extend the global `L`. Loading
them as parallel `<script>` tags would be a race condition — a
plugin script might execute before `leaflet.js` is parsed, leaving
`L` undefined. The pattern below loads each plugin dynamically from
inside the `<script>` block, so the order is guaranteed.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: render-heatmap
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."
assert 'markers' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the previous cell (Step 3a) first."

heatmap_map_id = f"ogham-heatmap-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
heatmap_data_var = f"__oghamHeatData_{heatmap_map_id.replace('-', '_')}"
heatmap_payload = json.dumps([[m["lat"], m["lon"], 0.7] for m in markers])

heatmap_html = f"""
<div id="{heatmap_map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px;margin-top:8px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{heatmap_data_var}"] = {heatmap_payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{heatmap_map_id}";
    const points = window["{heatmap_data_var}"];

    // Helper: load a Leaflet plugin script dynamically, after core Leaflet
    // is ready. Parallel <script> tags race; this pattern doesn't.
    function loadPluginWhenReady(src, readyCheck, cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && readyCheck()) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') {{
            return setTimeout(() => loadPluginWhenReady(src, readyCheck, cb), 50);
        }}
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = src;
        s.onload = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    function loadAllPlugins(cb) {{
        loadPluginWhenReady(
            'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.heat@0.2.0/dist/leaflet-heat.js',
            () => !!L.heatLayer,
            () => loadPluginWhenReady(
                'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js',
                () => !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen),
                cb
            )
        );
    }}

    let initAttempts = 0;
    const MAX_ATTEMPTS = 40;  // 40 × 50ms = 2 seconds grace for optional plugins

    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;

        // Heat plugin is required (no point rendering an empty map);
        // fullscreen is optional — wait briefly, then proceed without it.
        if (!L.heatLayer) return setTimeout(init, 50);
        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && initAttempts < MAX_ATTEMPTS) {{
            initAttempts++;
            return setTimeout(init, 50);
        }}

        // --- Base layers (one active at a time) ------------------------
        // URLs and attributions taken from the leaflet-providers registry:
        // https://github.com/leaflet-extras/leaflet-providers
        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors, '
                + 'Tiles style by <a href="https://www.hotosm.org/" target="_blank">Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team</a> '
                + 'hosted by <a href="https://openstreetmap.fr/" target="_blank">OpenStreetMap France</a>'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: Esri, i-cubed, USDA, USGS, AEX, GeoEye, '
                + 'Getmapping, Aerogrid, IGN, IGP, UPR-EGP, and the GIS User Community'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,  // Esri caps World_Terrain_Base at zoom 13
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // --- Overlay: density heatmap ----------------------------------
        const heatLayer = L.heatLayer(points, {{
            radius: 22, blur: 18, maxZoom: 11
        }});

        // --- Create map with OSM + heatmap + (optional) fullscreen ----
        const mapOptions = {{
            center: [53.0, -8.0], zoom: 7,
            layers: [osm, heatLayer]
        }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOptions.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOptions.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOptions);
        const bounds = points.map(p => [p[0], p[1]]);
        if (bounds.length) map.fitBounds(bounds, {{ padding: [30, 30] }});

        const baseLayers = {{
            "OpenStreetMap": osm,
            "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
            "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
            "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain
        }};
        const overlays = {{
            "Density heatmap": heatLayer
        }};
        // collapsed:true → Leaflet default, expands on hover / tap
        L.control.layers(baseLayers, overlays, {{ collapsed: true }}).addTo(map);

        // --- Fullscreen fix for the heatmap canvas ---------------------
        // leaflet.heat draws on a canvas sized at map-add time. When
        // the container resizes (entering or leaving fullscreen) the
        // canvas doesn't automatically repaint at the new size, so the
        // heatmap visually disappears. We hook into the fullscreen
        // plugin's `fullscreenchange` event, invalidate the map's size
        // so Leaflet re-measures, and force the heatLayer to redraw.
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            map.on('fullscreenchange', function() {{
                setTimeout(function() {{
                    map.invalidateSize();
                    if (map.hasLayer(heatLayer) && typeof heatLayer.redraw === 'function') {{
                        heatLayer.redraw();
                    }}
                }}, 200);
            }});
        }}

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadAllPlugins(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

_Html(heatmap_html)
```

## Step 4 — Exploring the data

The cells below are a free playground — filter the DataFrame by
county, compute per-county aggregates, or extend the query yourself.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: explore
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Example: list every Ogham stone from a given county, with coordinates.
# Change the filter below to explore other counties.
county_filter = "County Kerry"

subset = (
    df[df["countyLabel"] == county_filter]
      [["itemLabel", "siteLabel", "latitude", "longitude"]]
      .drop_duplicates()
      .reset_index(drop=True)
)
print(f"Ogham stones in {county_filter}: {len(subset)}")
subset
```

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| label: explore-stats
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Please run the data-loading cell first."

# Per-county summary: number of stones and the geographic centroid of
# their find-spots. The centroid is a quick sanity check — it should
# fall inside the county on the map.
summary = (
    df.groupby("countyLabel")
      .agg(
          n_stones=("itemLabel", "nunique"),
          n_sites=("siteLabel", "nunique"),
          mean_lat=("latitude", "mean"),
          mean_lon=("longitude", "mean"),
      )
      .sort_values("n_stones", ascending=False)
)
summary
```

---

*Part of an Open Educational Resource series on knowledge graphs and
linked open data, produced in the context of
[NFDI4Objects](https://www.nfdi4objects.net/).*
